training_summary: The corpus reveals limited direct military focus, with most works addressing broader classical and cultural topics. Key military insights come from Polybius commentary (#23), works on civil wars (#26, #32, #33), and general political/social context that frames military developments. The candidates represent core Republican military concepts spanning wars, battles, units, and command structures.

key_works.facet_contribution: Walbank's Polybius commentary provides detailed analysis of Roman military organization, tactics, and campaigns during the Middle Republic period
Lange's work on Hannibal to Sulla traces the militarization process and birth of civil war, showing how military conflicts transformed Republican institutions
Academic blog post examining civil war through ancient historical lens, providing modern scholarly perspective on Republican military conflicts
Hopkins' economic analysis includes military costs, taxation for wars, and economic impacts of conquest during Republican expansion

insights.label: Roman legion
insights.evidence: Polybius commentary discusses legion organization extensively; candidates include Fimbrian legions (Q60792542) showing specific legion types were significant
insights.reasoning: Core military unit missing from candidates but fundamental to all Republican warfare and military organization

insights.label: Marian reforms
insights.evidence: Lange's work on militarization discusses major military reforms; civil war context shows importance of professional army transformation
insights.reasoning: Critical transformation in Republican military history that professionalized the army and contributed to civil wars

insights.label: Pyrrhic War
insights.evidence: This war demonstrates Roman military adaptation against Hellenistic warfare tactics and represents early Republican expansion
insights.reasoning: Wars necessarily involve diplomatic aspects - alliances, treaties, negotiations with Greek states

insights.label: Praetorian Guard
insights.evidence: Listed as Republican candidate but Praetorian Guard was primarily Imperial institution; Republican equivalents were different bodyguard arrangements
insights.reasoning: Need to clarify Republican vs Imperial military institutions to avoid anachronistic concepts

candidate_assessments.label: Punic Wars
candidate_assessments.evidence: Core Republican military conflicts extensively referenced in Polybius commentary and civil war contexts

candidate_assessments.label: Pyrrhic War
candidate_assessments.evidence: Important early Republican military engagement showing adaptation to new warfare styles

candidate_assessments.label: Macedonian Wars
candidate_assessments.evidence: Key Republican expansion wars in Eastern Mediterranean, well-documented in classical sources

candidate_assessments.label: Social War of 91-87 BCE
candidate_assessments.evidence: Critical military conflict that preceded civil wars and demonstrated military recruitment issues

candidate_assessments.label: Gallic War
candidate_assessments.evidence: Major Republican conquest campaign with extensive documentation and military significance

candidate_assessments.label: Praetorian Guard
candidate_assessments.evidence: Primarily Imperial institution; Republican bodyguard arrangements were different and less formalized

candidate_assessments.label: Fimbrian legions
candidate_assessments.evidence: Specific Republican military units involved in civil wars, showing evolution of professional army

candidate_assessments.label: empire
candidate_assessments.evidence: Too broad and conceptual for Military facet; belongs in Political discussions of Republican expansion and governance

discovered_works.title: The Complete Roman Army
discovered_works.facet_contribution: Comprehensive overview of Roman military organization including Republican period

discovered_works.title: Infantry Combat in Livy's Battle Narratives
discovered_works.facet_contribution: Detailed analysis of Republican infantry tactics and combat based on primary source narratives

discovered_works.title: Religion and Classical Warfare: The Roman Republic
discovered_works.facet_contribution: Examines religious aspects of Republican military practices and rituals

decomposition_assessment.reason: Military facet covers distinct sub-domains each with substantial scholarly literature and concepts: wars/campaigns, organization/units, tactics/technology, command/strategy, civil conflicts, and naval warfare

sub_scopes.scope_description: Major wars, battles, and military campaigns of the Republic including Punic, Macedonian, Gallic, and Social Wars
sub_scopes.needs: Chronological sequencing, battle geography, commander prosopography, casualty analysis

sub_scopes.scope_description: Legion structure, unit types, recruitment, and military reforms throughout Republican period
sub_scopes.needs: Unit hierarchies, equipment catalogs, recruitment demographics, reform timelines

sub_scopes.scope_description: Internal military conflicts from Gracchi through Augustus including Sulla, Marius, Caesar
sub_scopes.needs: Factional analysis, veteran settlement patterns, constitutional impacts

sub_scopes.scope_description: Specific battles and military engagements with tactical and strategic significance
sub_scopes.needs: Tactical analysis, terrain studies, casualty records, source criticism

sub_scopes.scope_description: Military leadership, command structures, strategic planning, and general competency
sub_scopes.needs: Prosopographical data, command hierarchies, strategic doctrines, leadership analysis
